# Build the IDEA Brand Canvas (purpose → voice)

Setup: Rico is the operations VA for a busy apparel-brand owner. The owner told him "get the
brand canvas done" for the new sustainable-apparel launch (ASIN B0APP0002 — Avatar 2.0 already on
record). Rico has never built a brand canvas, doesn't know positioning vs value prop, and wants to
learn the *why* and do it right for the owner.

---

**Rico (User):** Hi! My boss asked me to "build the brand canvas" for our new sustainable apparel launch (ASIN B0APP0002). I've honestly never done this before and I want to do it right for her. Can you explain what a brand canvas even is, and then walk me through it step by step? I have plenty of time today.

**Coach:** Great attitude, and you've got time — so let's do it properly. First, what it is: the **IDEA Brand Canvas is a fill-in-the-boxes worksheet** that distills your customer avatar and the IDEA Framework into one simple brand-strategy document. The book's whole point is that brand strategy "doesn't have to be overwhelming" — instead of getting lost in complex exercises, the worksheet "guides you step by step to create a clear, compelling brand identity." So you're not inventing anything from scratch; you're filling in boxes in order. By the end you'll have eight statements: purpose, vision, mission, values, positioning, value proposition, personality, and voice.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/00-canvas-overview-and-instructions.md⟧

**Rico:** Okay that's reassuring. Is there an order I have to follow, or can I just start writing the purpose statement?

**Coach:** Good question — and the order matters a lot. The rule is **Avatar 2.0 → IDEA Framework → Brand Statements**, and the book is blunt: "the order is not optional." Here's the *why*: the first two tables (your avatar and the IDEA outputs) are the **raw material** the statements are built from. If you skip them and jump straight to writing a purpose, you get "generic, ungrounded output." So before we write a single statement, I'm going to load the avatar your boss already has on record — that's our input. One step-zero checklist for you to keep:

- [ ] Avatar 2.0 on record (the input)
- [ ] IDEA Framework lens understood (the four principles)
- [ ] THEN write the 8 brand statements (never before the two above)

Loading the avatar now.   ⟦tool: build_avatar⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/00-canvas-overview-and-instructions.md⟧

⟦tool: build_avatar — input {avatar_id: "av_B0APP0002", mode: "load"}⟧
→ avatar loaded: buyer_intent "avoid plastic/greenwashing in everyday basics"; motivation "identity/values-signaling, not lowest price"; emotional_triggers ["authenticity","fear of being deceived/greenwashed"]; shopper_type "Conscious"; demographics "US women 28–42, Millennial-skewing". lock=false, field_source=coach_extracted (server-set, not editable from here).

**Rico:** Got it — so that avatar is box one and I shouldn't overwrite it. Before we write statements, you mentioned "the IDEA Framework lens" and "the four principles." Can you explain what those are? I keep seeing "IDEA" and I don't know what the letters mean.

**Coach:** Yes — and this is the lens every statement gets checked against, so it's worth learning once. **IDEA = Insight-Driven, Distinctive, Empathetic, Authentic.** In plain language:

- **Insight-Driven** — understand your customer beyond demographics: what they feel, struggle with, value. "When customers feel understood, they are far more likely to engage and convert."
- **Distinctive** — stand out in a crowded market for the right reasons, so people choose you over competitors and remember you.
- **Empathetic** — connect on a human level; people "trust and stay loyal to brands that demonstrate genuine understanding."
- **Authentic** — be honest and consistent; "deliver on your promises." Skeptical customers spot fakes fast.

And the big idea behind all four: a brand isn't just a logo or an identity — **"it's a relationship between you and your customers."** Keep that frame; we're building a relationship, not decorating a label.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/00-overview-and-why-trust-wins/02-the-four-principles.md⟧

**Rico:** That actually makes sense. So every statement has to pass all four of those? Let me make sure I've got it: Insight = know the customer, Distinctive = stand out, Empathetic = connect emotionally, Authentic = be honest and keep promises. Right?

**Coach:** Exactly right — that's the checklist you'll run on every box. Now, before we write, I need one piece of raw material that isn't in the avatar: your brand's **"why"** — the founding reason it exists beyond just selling tees. Everything keys off that. Can you ask your boss (or tell me, if you know) the story of *why* she started this line? Even a few sentences.

**Rico:** I actually know this one — she's told the team. She was sick of "sustainable" basics that were either ugly, overpriced, or secretly fast fashion with a leaf slapped on the logo. She wanted a plain organic tee she could trust and wear every day. So: trustworthy everyday basics, sourcing you can verify, no greenwashing, made to last.

**Coach:** That's perfect raw material — and notice it already smells like all four principles (a real customer need, a stand-out promise, an emotional hook, and honesty). Let me capture it as structured context so every statement traces back to her actual words, not a template.   ⟦tool: run_conversation — input {message: "<Rico's relayed founding-why + brand promise>", avatar_context: "av_B0APP0002 sustainable apparel"}⟧

⟦tool: run_conversation⟧
→ extracted_context: { founding_why: "distrust of greenwashed/overpriced/fast-fashion basics", core_promise: "verifiable sourcing, no greenwashing, made-to-last everyday basics", emotional_spine: "trust + authenticity", category: "everyday sustainable apparel" }

**Coach:** Captured. Now we work through the eight statements **one at a time** — that's how the worksheet is designed. For each one I'll give you: (1) what it means, (2) a worked example from the book's eco-brand sample so you see the standard, (3) the fill-in for your brand, and (4) which IDEA principles to check. Sound good? Let's start with **Brand Purpose**.

**Rico:** Yes please, that structure is exactly what I need. What's a brand purpose?

**Coach:** **Brand Purpose = the "why" behind the brand's existence — the core reason it goes beyond just selling products.** It's *present-facing* (who you are now).

- *Worked example (book's eco sample):* "To inspire and empower individuals to embrace sustainable living, making eco-friendly choices accessible to everyone." Notice it's short and aspirational — hold yours to that brevity.
- *IDEA check:* Insight (names a need beyond the product) · Distinctive (a higher mission) · Empathetic (resonates with customer values) · Authentic (true to who you really are).
- *Your draft, from her "why":* **"To make trustworthy, sustainable basics people can wear every day without second-guessing — proving that 'eco' can mean honest, verifiable, and built to last."**

Quick understanding check: can you spot which word in your draft is the *Distinctive* part — the higher mission that sets it apart from other 'sustainable' brands?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/02-foundational-statements/00-brand-purpose.md⟧

**Rico:** I think it's "proving that 'eco' can mean honest" — that's the stand-out promise other brands aren't making. And the Empathetic part is "without second-guessing," because that's the feeling the customer wants. Did I get that right?

**Coach:** Spot on — you're reading the principles correctly now. Next: **Brand Vision.** Where purpose is *present*-facing, **vision is *future*-facing — an aspirational statement about the future impact the brand wants to make.** The trap to avoid: don't just restate the purpose in fancier words.

- *Worked example:* "To become the global leader in sustainable products, transforming the way people shop and live for a healthier planet." See how it's genuinely about the future?
- *IDEA check:* Distinctive (sets a unique direction for leadership) · Authentic (anchored to commitments you can actually honor).
- *Your draft:* **"To become the basics brand eco-conscious shoppers trust by default — making 'is this actually sustainable?' a question they never have to ask again."**

Understanding check: why is this a *vision* and not a *purpose*?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/02-foundational-statements/01-brand-vision.md⟧

**Rico:** Because it describes a future state — "trust by default," "never have to ask again" — that's where you're *going*, not just why you exist today. Purpose = why now, vision = where we're headed. Got it. What's next?

**Coach:** Perfect distinction. **Brand Mission = the actionable steps you take to fulfill the purpose and vision.** The tell: it should read as something the brand *does*, not just believes. The book's sample has three parts — an action, a beneficiary, and a without-compromise promise.

- *Worked example:* "To design innovative, eco-friendly solutions that help individuals and families reduce their environmental footprint without compromising quality or convenience." → action ("design… solutions") + beneficiary ("individuals and families") + without-compromise ("without compromising quality or convenience").
- *IDEA check:* Insight (specific customer need) · Distinctive (a unique way of delivering value).
- *Your draft:* **"To design verifiably-sourced organic everyday basics that last, publish the proof behind every claim, and make the trustworthy choice the easy one — without the greenwashing markup."**

Spot the three parts in yours — what's the action, the beneficiary, and the without-compromise promise?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/02-foundational-statements/02-brand-mission.md⟧

**Rico:** Action = "design verifiably-sourced organic basics… and publish the proof." Beneficiary = the everyday shopper who wants the trustworthy choice. Without-compromise = "without the greenwashing markup." This is clicking now. Next one?

**Coach:** You've got the pattern. **Brand Values = the guiding principles that shape how the brand treats customers, employees, and partners.** Big rule here: values are "only credible when they show up in action." So we don't list bare adjectives — we pair each value with a concrete commitment.

- *Worked example:* "Integrity, innovation, sustainability, and community. We commit to ethical practices, groundbreaking ideas, and fostering connections that benefit the planet and its people."
- *IDEA check:* the **Authentic** principle is the key test — "ensures the brand's actions consistently reflect its promises."
- *Your draft:* **"Transparency, durability, honesty, and craft. We publish our sourcing, we build basics that outlast trend cycles, we say what's true even when 'sustainable' would sell better, and we sweat the make."**

Homework-style check (this is the book's own test): for each of those four values, name one concrete thing the brand *already does* to prove it. If you (or your boss) can't, that value is aspirational, not real — flag it for her.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/02-foundational-statements/03-brand-values.md⟧

**Rico:** Okay: Transparency → they publish the supplier list. Durability → reinforced seams, a longevity guarantee. Honesty → they label exactly what's organic vs not. Craft → I'd have to confirm with her what we do for that one, so I'll flag "craft" as needs-proof. That's a really useful test.

**Coach:** That's exactly the right instinct — flagging "craft" instead of faking it *is* the Authentic principle in action. Now we move from the four foundational statements to the two **positioning** statements. First, a definition pair people mix up — you asked earlier about positioning vs value prop, so here's the clean line: **Positioning says *where you stand* (why you're the best choice). Value proposition says *why to buy* (the benefits).** We'll do positioning first.

**Positioning Statement = how the brand stands out in the market and why it's the best choice for the target audience.** It has a reliable shape: **"For [audience], our brand provides [offering] that [differentiator], [emotional payoff]."**

- *Worked example:* "For eco-conscious millennials, our brand provides stylish, sustainable clothing that combines innovative design with ethical practices, reflecting their values and empowering them to make a positive impact on the planet."
- *IDEA check:* Insight (addresses pain points) · Distinctive (the unique solution) · Empathetic (emotional aspiration) · Authentic (real brand strengths).
- *Your draft (audience pulled straight from av_B0APP0002):* **"For eco-conscious women who are tired of greenwashed basics, our brand makes verifiably-sourced everyday apparel that's built to last — so they can trust what they wear and stop second-guessing 'sustainable.'"**

Check: can you label the four slots — For [audience] / provides [offering] / that [differentiator] / [emotional payoff] — in your draft?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/03-positioning-statements/00-positioning-statement.md⟧

**Rico:** For [eco-conscious women tired of greenwashed basics] / provides [verifiably-sourced everyday apparel] / that's [built to last] / [so they can trust what they wear and stop second-guessing]. Four slots, all filled. And the audience came from the avatar, not a guess — that's the sequencing rule paying off. Now the value proposition — that's "why to buy," right?

**Coach:** Right. **Value Proposition = a clear statement of why a customer should buy from you instead of a competitor.** The key test here is the **Empathetic** principle: it must cover **both practical AND emotional benefits.** A value prop that lists only features fails the emotional half; one with only feelings fails the practical half.

- *Worked example:* "Our innovative product line combines modern aesthetics, affordability, and eco-friendly materials to provide guilt-free solutions for everyday living." → practical (aesthetics, affordability, materials) + emotional ("guilt-free").
- *Your draft:* **"Verifiable organic sourcing, made-to-last construction, and zero greenwashing — basics you can finally wear without the guilt or the doubt."**

Check: point to the practical part and the emotional part in yours.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/03-positioning-statements/01-value-proposition.md⟧

**Rico:** Practical = "verifiable organic sourcing, made-to-last construction, zero greenwashing." Emotional = "without the guilt or the doubt." Both halves, so it passes the Empathetic test. Two left — personality and voice. What's the difference between those? They sound like the same thing to me.

**Coach:** Common confusion, and there's a clean rule: **personality is *who the brand is* (its traits); voice is *how it speaks* (the words).** Personality is the input; voice is the output. So we do personality first.

**Brand Personality = the human-like traits that influence how the brand speaks, interacts, and resonates.** The book's tip: name the **traits AND an archetype/role** — a bare adjective list is hard to act on, but pairing it with a role makes it usable.

- *Worked example:* "Warm, innovative, approachable, and bold. Our brand is the friendly guide helping customers transition to a sustainable lifestyle, one simple choice at a time." → four traits + a role ("the friendly guide").
- *Your draft:* **"Honest, grounded, quietly confident, and exacting. Your brand is the straight-talking insider who shows the receipts — never the cheerleader, never the lecturer."**

Check: what's the archetype/role in yours, separate from the trait list?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/04-expression-statements/00-brand-personality.md⟧

**Rico:** Traits = "honest, grounded, quietly confident, exacting." Role = "the straight-talking insider who shows the receipts." So now voice is how that insider actually talks?

**Coach:** Exactly — voice expresses the personality in words. **Brand Voice = the tone, language, and communication style used across every platform.** Write it *after* personality is settled (which we just did). The book's sample gives a tone trio plus a "how we speak" sentence — and there's an operational test from the **Authentic** principle: the voice must be **consistent "across marketing, sales, and support"** — it has to hold up in a returns email, not just a hero headline.

- *Worked example:* "Optimistic, conversational, and empowering. We speak with clarity and enthusiasm, making sustainability feel exciting and accessible to all."
- *Your draft:* **"Plain-spoken, evidence-first, and warm-but-no-fluff. We state the claim, then the proof — and we'd write a returns email in the same voice we write a hero headline."**

Note the part "we'd write a returns email in the same voice we write a hero headline" — that's the consistency test built right in. That's all eight statements.   ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/04-expression-statements/01-brand-voice.md⟧

**Rico:** That's all eight! Can you now put them all together into one document I can hand my boss? And earlier you mentioned the statements should be "coherent" — how do I check that?

**Coach:** Great instinct — that's the final and most important check. The completed canvas should be treated as **one consolidated document, not a stack of separate boxes**: purpose ↔ vision ↔ mission ↔ values ↔ voice should all "tell the same story." The reason it matters: this finished canvas is "the core document you'll use to train AI on your brand and even create your own BrandGPT" — and "a coherent canvas produces a coherent AI; a fragmented one produces fragmented output." Let me export it as one document now.   ⟦tool: export_strategy_doc — input {avatar_id: "av_B0APP0002", brand_context: "<extracted_context>", section: "brand_canvas_statements", format: "doc"}⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/02-brand/01-brand-canvas/04-expression-statements/02-canvas-brings-strategy-to-life.md⟧

⟦tool: export_strategy_doc⟧
→ strategy doc generated: 8 brand statements (purpose, vision, mission, values, positioning, value_proposition, personality, voice); idempotent per avatar snapshot.
⟦iv-os: log_asset — brand-canvas doc linked to B0APP0002⟧ *(mocked: no canon/asset data invented by coach)*

**Coach:** Done. The full document is below, plus a reusable checklist so you can run this yourself for the boss's next product.

### Artifact produced

**IDEA Brand Canvas — B0APP0002 (sustainable apparel) — DRAFT for owner review**

| # | Statement | What it means (plain language) | Your statement (drafted) | IDEA check + grounding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Brand Purpose** | The "why" the brand exists, beyond selling products. *Present-facing.* | "To make trustworthy, sustainable basics people can wear every day without second-guessing — proving that 'eco' can mean honest, verifiable, and built to last." | Need beyond the tee (Insight) · higher mission vs greenwashing (Distinctive) · anti-deception values (Empathetic) · true to the founding story (Authentic). Held to the sample's brevity. — `02-foundational-statements/00-brand-purpose.md` |
| 2 | **Brand Vision** | Aspirational future impact. *Future-facing — not a restated purpose.* | "To become the basics brand eco-conscious shoppers trust by default — making 'is this actually sustainable?' a question they never have to ask again." | Genuinely future-oriented; unique direction (Distinctive); a commitment the brand can honor (Authentic). — `02-foundational-statements/01-brand-vision.md` |
| 3 | **Brand Mission** | The actionable steps that fulfill purpose + vision. *What the brand DOES.* | "To design verifiably-sourced organic everyday basics that last, publish the proof behind every claim, and make the trustworthy choice the easy one — without the greenwashing markup." | Action + beneficiary + without-compromise promise, like the worked sample. — `02-foundational-statements/02-brand-mission.md` |
| 4 | **Brand Values** | Guiding principles — credible only when shown in action. | "Transparency, durability, honesty, and craft. We publish our sourcing, we build basics that outlast trend cycles, we say what's true even when 'sustainable' would sell better, and we sweat the make." | Each value paired with a concrete commitment (Authentic = actions reflect promises). *Rico flagged "craft" as needs-proof — confirm with owner.* — `02-foundational-statements/03-brand-values.md` |
| 5 | **Positioning Statement** | How you stand out + why you're the best choice. *"For [audience]…" shape.* | "For eco-conscious women who are tired of greenwashed basics, our brand makes verifiably-sourced everyday apparel that's built to last — so they can trust what they wear and stop second-guessing 'sustainable.'" | For-audience / offering / differentiator / emotional-payoff structure; audience pulled from av_B0APP0002. — `03-positioning-statements/00-positioning-statement.md` |
| 6 | **Value Proposition** | Why buy from you vs a competitor. *Practical AND emotional.* | "Verifiable organic sourcing, made-to-last construction, and zero greenwashing — basics you can finally wear without the guilt or the doubt." | Stacks practical value AND lands an emotional payoff — passes both halves of the Empathetic test. — `03-positioning-statements/01-value-proposition.md` |
| 7 | **Brand Personality** | The human-like traits + an archetype. *The input to voice.* | "Honest, grounded, quietly confident, and exacting. Your brand is the straight-talking insider who shows the receipts — never the cheerleader, never the lecturer." | Traits AND a role/archetype so it's usable, not just adjectives. — `04-expression-statements/00-brand-personality.md` |
| 8 | **Brand Voice** | Tone, language, style across every platform. *Expresses the personality.* | "Plain-spoken, evidence-first, and warm-but-no-fluff. We state the claim, then the proof — and we'd write a returns email in the same voice we write a hero headline." | Tone trio + how-we-speak line; passes the Authentic test (holds up in support, not just a headline). Written after personality. — `04-expression-statements/01-brand-voice.md` |

**Coherence note (the canvas is ONE document):** purpose ("wear without second-guessing") → vision ("never have to ask again") → mission ("publish the proof") → values ("transparency") → positioning/value-prop ("verifiable, no greenwashing") → personality ("shows the receipts") → voice ("claim, then proof") all run on one spine: **trust through verifiable honesty.** That's what makes it a single source of truth you can later use to train an AI / build a BrandGPT (Chapter 7).

**Reusable checklist — "Build a Brand Canvas" (for Rico to reuse on the owner's next product):**
1. [ ] Confirm Avatar 2.0 is on record FIRST (sequencing rule — order is not optional).
2. [ ] Capture the founding "why" from the owner (raw material for every statement).
3. [ ] Learn/apply the IDEA lens: Insight · Distinctive · Empathetic · Authentic — run all four on every box.
4. [ ] Write the 8 statements **in order**: purpose → vision → mission → values → positioning → value prop → personality → voice.
5. [ ] Per statement, check it against its IDEA principles (and for Values, name a concrete proof for each).
6. [ ] Check coherence — do all 8 tell the same story? Flag anything off-spine.
7. [ ] Export as ONE document; send to owner for review; flag any "needs-proof" values.

**Next step for Rico:** Send this to your boss for review. Two things to confirm with her: (a) the "craft" value needs a concrete proof point, and (b) whether the personality/voice (the "straight-talking insider") feels right to her. Once she signs off, this finished canvas is what you'd upload to train a brand AI.

### Assertions (oracle — machine-checkable)

- [skill-faithful] "fill-in-the-boxes worksheet… guides you step by step" + the eight-statement scope trace to `00-canvas-overview-and-instructions.md`.
- [skill-faithful] "Avatar 2.0 → IDEA Framework → Brand Statements" and "the order is not optional" (skipping yields generic/ungrounded output) trace to `00-canvas-overview-and-instructions.md`.
- [skill-faithful] The four principles taught to Rico (Insight-Driven / Distinctive / Empathetic / Authentic) and "a brand is a relationship between you and your customers, not just an identity" trace to `00-overview-and-why-trust-wins/02-the-four-principles.md`.
- [skill-faithful] Each statement's definition, worked example, and IDEA check traces to its element skill: purpose="why beyond products, present-facing, brevity" → `00-brand-purpose.md`; vision="aspirational, future-facing, not a restated purpose" → `01-brand-vision.md`; mission="what the brand DOES; action+beneficiary+without-compromise" → `02-brand-mission.md`; values="credible only in action; name a concrete proof per value" → `03-brand-values.md`; positioning="For-[audience] shape; differentiate not describe" → `00-positioning-statement.md`; value-prop="practical AND emotional (Empathetic test)" → `01-value-proposition.md`; personality="traits + archetype, input to voice" → `00-brand-personality.md`; voice="tone trio + how-we-speak, holds up in a support reply" → `01-brand-voice.md`.
- [skill-faithful] The worked examples quoted to Rico are the book's verbatim eco-brand samples (e.g. purpose "To inspire and empower individuals to embrace sustainable living…", voice "Optimistic, conversational, and empowering…") — no invented examples.
- [skill-faithful] "treat the canvas as one consolidated document; coherent canvas → coherent AI; the canvas is the core document to train AI / build a BrandGPT" traces to `04-expression-statements/02-canvas-brings-strategy-to-life.md`.
- [persona-adapt:P2] Teaching + scaffolding: coach DEFINED every term before applying it (brand canvas, IDEA letters, positioning vs value prop, personality vs voice), gave a worked example per statement, used a step-by-step one-at-a-time walkthrough, ran repeated understanding checks ("which word is the Distinctive part?", "why is this a vision not a purpose?", "label the four slots"), and produced a step-zero checklist + a reusable "Build a Brand Canvas" checklist + a fill-in template structure — leveraging Rico's abundant time. Visibly different from the P1 compressed "just draft it" delivery.
- [artifact] A fully drafted 8-statement IDEA Brand Canvas (purpose → voice) is present, plus a reusable checklist; each statement edit-ready and grounded; "craft" value flagged as needs-proof.
- [safety] No cross-tenant data; no invented reviews/stats/canon — IV-OS `log_asset` shown as an `⟦iv-os:⟧` mock, never invented as real data; the avatar input is the owner's own on-record av_B0APP0002 with server-set `field_source` preserved (not editable from MCP args).
